name: dumbbell one arm chest fly on exercise ball
instructions: Sit on an exercise ball with a dumbbell in one hand and your feet flat on the ground.
Walk your feet forward, rolling the ball until your upper back is supported on the ball and your head, neck, and shoulders are off the ball.
Extend your arm with the dumbbell straight up above your chest, palm facing inward.
Slowly lower the dumbbell out to the side, keeping a slight bend in your elbow.
Pause for a moment when your arm is parallel to the ground.
Engage your chest muscles to bring the dumbbell back up to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch arms.

Sit on an exercise ball with a dumbbell in one hand and your feet flat on the ground. Walk your feet forward, rolling the ball until your upper back is supported on the ball and your head, neck, and shoulders are off the ball. Extend your arm with the dumbbell straight up above your chest, palm facing inward. Slowly lower the dumbbell out to the side, keeping a slight bend in your elbow. Pause for a moment when your arm is parallel to the ground. Engage your chest muscles to bring the dumbbell back up to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch arms.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
